cards.definition: the Christian belief that God exists as three persons in one — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit
the belief that God became human in the person of Jesus Christ
being saved from sin — Christians believe Jesus' death and resurrection made salvation possible
the belief that Jesus rose from the dead three days after the crucifixion — central to Christian faith
the death of Jesus on the cross — seen as an atonement for human sin
the idea that Jesus' death on the cross repaired the relationship between humanity and God
the Christian belief in eternal life with God after death — for those who have been saved
in Christianity — eternal separation from God — punishment for those who reject God
communication with God — personal prayer, liturgical prayer, intercessory prayer
sacred rites in Christianity — baptism and Holy Communion (Eucharist) are recognised by most denominations

questions.question: What is the Trinity?
options: A) The three books of the Bible
B) God as three persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit
C) Three Christian sacraments
D) Three Christian denominations
questions.explanation: The Trinity: God is one being but three persons — Father (creator), Son (Jesus), Holy Spirit (guide). It is central to Christian theology.

questions.question: What is the Incarnation?
options: A) Jesus' resurrection
B) God becoming human in Jesus Christ
C) The Holy Spirit entering believers
D) Jesus' baptism
questions.explanation: The Incarnation: God took on human flesh in the person of Jesus. 'The Word became flesh' (John 1:14).

questions.question: Why is the resurrection central to Christianity?
options: A) It proves Jesus was God
B) Without it, faith would be pointless — it confirms victory over death and sin
C) It fulfils Old Testament prophecy only
D) It established the Church
questions.explanation: St Paul wrote: 'If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile.' The resurrection confirms salvation and eternal life.

questions.question: What is atonement?
options: A) Prayer to God for forgiveness
B) Going to heaven after death
C) Jesus' death restoring the broken relationship between humanity and God
D) Baptism washing away sin
questions.explanation: Atonement: Jesus' sacrificial death on the cross repaired ('atoned for') the damage done by human sin, reconciling humanity with God.

questions.question: What is salvation?
options: A) Saying prayers in church
B) Being saved from sin and its consequences — eternal separation from God
C) Baptism in the Church
D) Following the Ten Commandments
questions.explanation: Salvation: Christians believe Jesus' death and resurrection offer humans forgiveness of sin and the possibility of eternal life with God.

questions.question: What are the sacraments?
options: A) Sacred texts in the Bible
B) Christian holy days
C) Sacred rites — including baptism and Holy Communion
D) Christian prayer forms
questions.explanation: Sacraments are sacred rituals — Catholics recognise seven; most Protestants recognise two (baptism and Eucharist).

questions.question: What does the crucifixion represent?
options: A) God's abandonment of Jesus
B) The beginning of Jesus' ministry
C) Jesus' death as a sacrifice atoning for human sin
D) The founding of the Christian Church
questions.explanation: The crucifixion is seen as the ultimate sacrifice — Jesus died to take the punishment for human sin, making salvation possible.

questions.question: What is heaven in Christian belief?
options: A) A state of mind
B) Eternal life in the presence of God after death
C) The afterlife for all people
D) A physical place above the clouds
questions.explanation: Heaven: Christians believe those saved through faith will spend eternity in God's presence — a state of perfect peace and joy.

questions.question: What is liturgical prayer?
options: A) Silent, private prayer
B) Formal, set prayers used in church worship
C) Prayer through fasting
D) Personal prayer at home
questions.explanation: Liturgical prayer uses set words and forms — e.g. the Lord's Prayer, prayers in church services. It is communal and structured.

questions.question: What is baptism?
options: A) A private prayer of commitment
B) The ceremony of Holy Communion
C) A sacrament using water — entry into the Christian community
D) A Christian holy day
questions.explanation: Baptism uses water to symbolise washing away sin and entry into the Christian community. Infant and adult (believer's) baptism are both practised.

questions.question: The Trinity means God is three separate gods.
questions.explanation: The Trinity: God is ONE being in three persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Not three gods.

questions.question: The Incarnation is the belief that God became human in Jesus.
questions.explanation: 'The Word became flesh' — Jesus is believed to be fully God and fully human.

questions.question: The resurrection is central to Christian faith.
questions.explanation: St Paul said: if Christ has not been raised, faith is futile. The resurrection confirms victory over death.

questions.question: Atonement means going to heaven after death.
questions.explanation: Atonement means Jesus' death repaired the broken relationship between humanity and God.

questions.question: Christians believe salvation is possible through Jesus.
questions.explanation: Jesus' death and resurrection are believed to offer forgiveness of sin and eternal life.

questions.question: Most Protestant denominations recognise seven sacraments.
questions.explanation: Most Protestants recognise only two sacraments — baptism and the Eucharist. Catholics recognise seven.

questions.question: The crucifixion represents Jesus' triumphant entry into Jerusalem.
questions.explanation: The crucifixion is Jesus' death on the cross — seen as a sacrifice atoning for human sin.

questions.question: Heaven is eternal separation from God.
questions.explanation: Heaven is eternal UNION with God. Hell is eternal separation from God.

questions.question: Baptism symbolises washing away sin and entry into the Christian community.
questions.explanation: Baptism uses water as a symbol of spiritual cleansing and new life in Christ.

questions.question: Liturgical prayer uses set words in communal worship.
questions.explanation: Liturgical prayer includes the Lord's Prayer and set forms used in church services.

questions.question: The ________ is the belief that God is three persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
questions.explanation: God is one being in three persons — a central and distinctive Christian belief.

questions.question: The ________ is the belief that God became human in Jesus Christ.
questions.explanation: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14).

questions.question: Jesus' death on the cross is called the ________.
questions.explanation: The crucifixion is seen as a sacrifice atoning for human sin.

questions.question: ________ is the idea that Jesus' death restored the relationship between humanity and God.
questions.explanation: Without atonement, Christians believe humanity would remain separated from God by sin.

questions.question: ________ means being saved from sin — made possible through Jesus.
questions.explanation: Christians believe faith in Jesus and his resurrection offers eternal salvation.

questions.question: The ________ — Jesus rising from the dead — is central to Christian faith.
questions.explanation: Without the resurrection, St Paul says, Christian faith is 'futile'.

questions.question: ________ is eternal life with God — for those who have been saved.
questions.explanation: Christians believe heaven is a state of perfect peace and joy in God's presence.

questions.question: ________ is seen as eternal separation from God.
questions.explanation: Hell represents the ultimate consequence of rejecting God.

questions.question: ________ is communication with God — personal, liturgical, or intercessory.
questions.explanation: Prayer is fundamental to Christian life — direct communication with God.

questions.question: ________ and baptism are the two sacraments recognised by most Protestant denominations.
questions.explanation: The Eucharist (Holy Communion) commemorates the Last Supper.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: God as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — one being, three persons
God became human in Jesus Christ
Jesus' death — sacrifice for human sin
Jesus' death restoring relationship between humanity and God
Jesus rising from the dead — central to faith

cards.definition: the oneness of God — the central belief in Islam — Allah is one, unique, and indivisible
the five core duties of Muslim life: Shahadah, Salah, Zakah, Sawm, Hajj
the declaration of faith: 'There is no God but Allah and Muhammad is his messenger'
obligatory prayer — five times daily — facing Mecca
giving 2.5% of surplus wealth to those in need — one of the Five Pillars
fasting during Ramadan — no food, drink, or smoking from dawn to sunset
the pilgrimage to Mecca — required once in a lifetime if able — one of the Five Pillars
the holy book of Islam — believed to be the direct word of God, revealed to Muhammad
the final prophet of Islam — received revelations from God via the angel Jibril (Gabriel)
life after death in Islam — Day of Judgement, heaven (Jannah), and hell (Jahannam)

questions.question: What is Tawhid?
options: A) The belief in many gods
B) The belief in the oneness and uniqueness of God (Allah)
C) The five duties of Muslims
D) The holy book of Islam
questions.explanation: Tawhid is the most fundamental belief in Islam — Allah is one, unique, and has no partners or equals. Shirk (associating others with God) is the greatest sin.

questions.question: What are the Five Pillars of Islam?
options: A) Qur'an, prayer, fasting, hajj, charity
B) Shahadah, Salah, Zakah, Sawm, Hajj
C) Prayer, fasting, pilgrimage, zakat, jihad
D) Faith, hope, charity, prayer, pilgrimage
questions.explanation: The Five Pillars: Shahadah (declaration), Salah (prayer), Zakah (charity), Sawm (fasting), Hajj (pilgrimage).

questions.question: What is the Shahadah?
options: A) Prayer five times daily
B) Fasting during Ramadan
C) The declaration of faith: no God but Allah and Muhammad is his messenger
D) Pilgrimage to Mecca
questions.explanation: The Shahadah: 'La ilaha illallah, Muhammadur rasulullah.' It is the first Pillar — saying it with sincerity makes a person Muslim.

questions.question: How many times do Muslims pray (Salah) each day?
options: A) Three
B) Seven
C) Once
D) Five
questions.explanation: Muslims perform Salah five times daily — dawn, midday, afternoon, sunset, and night — facing Mecca.

questions.question: What is Zakah?
options: A) Fasting during Ramadan
B) Pilgrimage to Mecca
C) Giving 2.5% of surplus wealth to those in need
D) Saying prayers five times daily
questions.explanation: Zakah: 2.5% of surplus wealth given annually to the poor. It purifies wealth and promotes social justice.

questions.question: What is Sawm?
options: A) Prayer
B) Fasting during Ramadan — no food or drink from dawn to sunset
C) Charitable giving
D) Pilgrimage
questions.explanation: Sawm is fasting during the month of Ramadan. It involves spiritual reflection, self-discipline, and gratitude.

questions.question: What is the Qur'an believed to be?
options: A) Muhammad's own writings
B) A collection of hadith
C) The direct word of God, revealed to Muhammad
D) The Torah adapted for Islam
questions.explanation: The Qur'an is believed to be the literal word of Allah, revealed to Muhammad through the angel Jibril over 23 years.

questions.question: What is akhirah?
options: A) The Five Pillars
B) The oneness of God
C) Life after death — Day of Judgement, Jannah, and Jahannam
D) The pilgrimage to Mecca
questions.explanation: Akhirah is the Islamic concept of afterlife — all souls will be judged on the Day of Judgement and sent to Jannah (paradise) or Jahannam (hell).

questions.question: What is Hajj?
options: A) Fasting during Ramadan
B) The declaration of faith
C) Charitable giving
D) Pilgrimage to Mecca — required once in a lifetime
questions.explanation: Hajj is the annual pilgrimage to Mecca — required of every Muslim who is physically and financially able. It is the fifth Pillar.

questions.question: Who was the Prophet Muhammad?
options: A) The founder of Judaism
B) The first prophet
C) The final prophet of Islam who received God's revelations
D) A religious scholar who wrote the Qur'an
questions.explanation: Muhammad (pbuh) is the final prophet — God's last messenger. He received the Qur'an through the angel Jibril between 610–632 CE.

questions.question: Tawhid means God is three persons in Islam.
questions.explanation: Tawhid means God is ONE — indivisible and unique. This is the fundamental difference from the Christian Trinity.

questions.question: The Five Pillars are the core duties of Muslim life.
questions.explanation: The Five Pillars — Shahadah, Salah, Zakah, Sawm, Hajj — structure Muslim worship and life.

questions.question: The Shahadah is the declaration of faith.
questions.explanation: Saying the Shahadah sincerely is the entry point to Islam.

questions.question: Muslims pray three times daily.
questions.explanation: Muslims perform Salah FIVE times daily — at dawn, midday, afternoon, sunset, and night.

questions.question: Zakah is fasting during Ramadan.
questions.explanation: Zakah is charitable giving — 2.5% of surplus wealth. Sawm is fasting.

questions.question: The Qur'an is believed to be Muhammad's own words.
questions.explanation: The Qur'an is believed to be the direct word of Allah, revealed to Muhammad through the angel Jibril.

questions.question: Hajj must be performed once in a lifetime by those able to.
questions.explanation: Hajj is the fifth Pillar — compulsory for all Muslims who are physically and financially capable.

questions.question: Akhirah refers to life after death in Islam.
questions.explanation: Akhirah includes the Day of Judgement, Jannah (paradise), and Jahannam (hell).

questions.question: Sawm means giving 2.5% of surplus wealth to the poor.
questions.explanation: Sawm is fasting during Ramadan. Zakah is charitable giving.

questions.question: Shirk (associating partners with God) is the greatest sin in Islam.
questions.explanation: Tawhid (God's oneness) is fundamental — shirk contradicts it and is considered the gravest sin.

questions.question: ________ is the Islamic belief in the absolute oneness of God.
questions.explanation: Tawhid is the most fundamental belief — Allah is one, unique, with no partners.

questions.question: The Five ________ are the core duties of Muslim life.
questions.explanation: Shahadah, Salah, Zakah, Sawm, Hajj.

questions.question: The ________ is the declaration of faith: 'No God but Allah and Muhammad is his messenger'.
questions.explanation: The Shahadah is the first Pillar and the entry point into Islam.

questions.question: ________ is obligatory prayer five times daily, facing Mecca.
questions.explanation: Salah is performed at dawn, midday, afternoon, sunset, and night.

questions.question: ________ is giving 2.5% of surplus wealth to those in need.
questions.explanation: Zakah promotes social justice and purifies wealth.

questions.question: ________ is fasting during Ramadan — no food or drink from dawn to sunset.
questions.explanation: Sawm develops self-discipline, gratitude, and empathy for the poor.

questions.question: ________ is the pilgrimage to Mecca — required once if able.
questions.explanation: Hajj is the fifth Pillar — performed in the Islamic month of Dhu al-Hijjah.

questions.question: The ________ is the holy book of Islam — the direct word of Allah.
questions.explanation: The Qur'an was revealed to Muhammad through the angel Jibril over 23 years.

questions.question: ________ is the concept of life after death in Islam.
questions.explanation: Akhirah includes the Day of Judgement, Jannah (paradise), and Jahannam (hell).

questions.question: Muhammad is the ________ prophet of Islam.
questions.explanation: Muslims believe Muhammad was the last prophet — completing God's revelation.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: oneness of God — fundamental Islamic belief
2.5% of surplus wealth given to those in need
fasting during Ramadan — dawn to sunset
pilgrimage to Mecca — once in a lifetime
